title: Net Mending
desc: A torn silk panel has to be repaired before dawn or the morning yield is lost.

steps.text:
You reach the dew farm while the stars are still visible. In the dark, the nets look like empty frames hung between stone pillars. Up close, one of them is ruined. Net eleven has split along the lower third - not a total failure, but enough that the first hour of condensation will pour straight through if no one fixes it.

The net-keeper kneels beside the tear with a bundle of silk thread in her lap and a glass hook clenched between her teeth. "Night gust, maybe. Maybe a dune glider got thirsty and landed where it shouldn't. Doesn't matter now." She spits the hook into her hand and points up the ladder rig. "We patch the seam, or we retension the whole lower line. Either way, it has to be catching dew before first light."

The air is cold enough to sting. The rest of the field waits in perfect stillness.

options.label: Climb the ladder rig and patch the tear directly
Reset the lower support line before repairing anything

steps.text:
The silk is finer than fabric and less forgiving. From the ladder, you can see each strand where the tear pulled it apart - some snapped clean, others stretched into a translucent fringe. You feed the thread through with the glass hook, tying the seam in tiny mirrored loops while the net-keeper below keeps the panel steady with both hands.

The first time you pull too hard, the whole edge sings in protest. After that you slow down. Stitch, tighten, breathe. Stitch, tighten, breathe. By the time you finish the last span, the eastern horizon has gone from black to charcoal.

Then the dew starts to form. Tiny beads gather along the repaired seam, hesitate, and hold. A thin runnel forms, then another, trickling down toward the collection channel below. The net-keeper touches the patched section with the back of her fingers and nods once. "Good. Ugly repair. Strong repair. That's the right order."

options.label: Watch the repaired seam begin to catch water

steps.text:
The lower line has slipped half an inch out of alignment. That is enough. At the dew farm, half an inch can become an entire morning's loss. You unhook the anchor pin while the net-keeper braces the frame, then drag the tension line back into position a finger-width at a time. The work is heavier than it looks. Silk weighs almost nothing; wet silk and stone balance systems do not.

Your palms burn by the time the line sits flush against the marker notch again. Only then do you repair the torn section, the seam lying flatter now that the whole panel is under proper tension. When the first condensation gathers, it does not sag or spill. It travels cleanly down the face of the net in silver threads.

The net-keeper studies the line, then the notch, then the way the droplets are moving. "There. That's how it's supposed to look. Most people think water work is about water. It's really about angles." She sounds pleased, which in a Mipedian work yard is almost applause.

options.label: Step back and admire the corrected line
